title: Runtime Performance Architecture
use_for: designing CPU-first performance architecture for custom runtimes
moving heavy Unity-hosted work into deterministic runtime phases
coordinating storage layout, commands, scheduler, synchronization, and diagnostics
building performance models around real workloads rather than isolated micro-optimizations
do_not_use_for: single local micro-optimizations with no runtime boundary
GPU pipeline architecture as the primary concern
UI composition as the primary concern
rules: treat performance as a runtime contract, not an after-the-fact patch
preserve correctness, architectural clarity, and determinism before throughput
keep Unity or other host APIs outside worker and core runtime contracts
define phase, command, storage, scheduler, synchronization, and diagnostics boundaries before tuning
keep single-thread execution as the correctness and debugging baseline
grow multithreading only from validated workload shape and ownership
procedure: Identify the runtime identity, host boundary, and real workload being optimized.
Map lifecycle phases and mutation windows.
Map storage ownership, handles, packed indices, snapshots, write state, and published state.
Map task categories: serial, range, tiled, grouped, island, reduction, and barrier.
Define synchronization boundaries: capture, compute, barrier, commit/publish, submit.
Define diagnostics: phase timings, task timings, worker utilization, storage counters, queue/backlog, and validation errors.
Choose optimization pressure points from evidence before modifying scheduler, storage, or workload shape.

primary_when: the request is about performance architecture of a custom runtime, simulation substrate, CPU task system, Unity-hosted runtime, phase pipeline, storage/scheduler design, or deterministic multithreading
the task needs coordination between scheduling, storage, synchronization, work budgeting, and profiling
must_not_own_when: the request is only about a narrow cache, allocation, or scheduler issue already owned by a more specific performance skill
the primary concern is GPU-driven rendering or compute pipeline architecture
the request is mainly about UI, plugin lifecycle, or assembly parsing

solves: how to make runtime performance coherent across lifecycle, phases, storage, scheduler, synchronization, and diagnostics
how to optimize Unity-hosted heavy computation without letting Unity objects leak into worker execution
how to keep multithreaded CPU performance deterministic and measurable
how to validate performance against real simulation workloads and synthetic stress probes
does_not_solve: GPU pipeline design as the main architecture
adopting Unity DOTS, ECS, Jobs, or third-party frameworks as the default solution

core_principles: Runtime performance starts with responsibility boundaries.
A phase is a synchronization and mutation contract, not a label.
Commands describe mutation intent; storage applies mutation at controlled boundaries.
Packed storage separates stable identity from dense iteration location.
Worker tasks need explicit read/write ownership, not access to host objects.
Single-thread mode remains a permanent correctness, profiling, and debugging asset.
Parallel speedup is valid only after snapshot, barrier, commit, and publish rules are clear.
Diagnostics should observe the runtime without becoming hidden execution policy.
constraints: Do not bypass command boundaries for convenience in performance-sensitive runtime code.
Do not perform structural mutation from worker tasks.
Do not let core runtime depend on Unity types, GameObjects, Transforms, renderers, or host state.
Do not add scheduler sophistication before workload shape, ownership, and measurement justify it.
Do not optimize for average throughput while ignoring frame spikes, backlog, and tail latency.
Do not allow optional integrations such as rendering or GPU paths to define the core runtime identity.
decision_rules: Use runtime-performance-architecture when several performance concerns meet: phases, storage, scheduler, synchronization, diagnostics, and host boundary.
Attach scheduler when task granularity, dispatch mode, chunking, worker utilization, barriers, or reductions are in scope.
Attach data-oriented-design when packed storage, hot/cold layout, handle/index separation, or range traversal is in scope.
Attach measurement-profiling whenever the bottleneck is not yet evidence-backed.
Attach contention-shaping when scaling collapses around shared writes, locks, queues, or reductions.
Attach work-budgeting when frame responsiveness, backlog, burst workloads, or phase budgets are in scope.
Attach memory-arenas when phase-local scratch, transient buffers, or reset-at-boundary memory is in scope.

preferred_patterns: small public lifecycle facade with explicit runtime core behind it
phase pipeline with collect, lifecycle, compute, prepare, submit, and diagnostics boundaries
command queues for mutation intent and controlled application windows
packed storage with handles, slot reuse, generation checks, dense iteration, and diagnostics
range, tiled, grouped, island, reduction, and barrier task categories
Unity host adapters that consume published state instead of controlling compute order
synthetic stress modules plus realistic workload probes for scheduler validation
avoid_patterns: one giant runtime manager that owns lifecycle, domain logic, storage, scheduling, and rendering
worker tasks reading Unity objects or host-owned mutable state
structural mutation hidden inside compute tasks
object-per-entity hot paths when dense storage and ranges are available
scheduler complexity that outgrows validated workload needs
diagnostics that force synchronization or change the execution model by default

recipes.name: Review a CPU-first runtime for performance readiness
recipes.when: Use when evaluating or designing a custom runtime that should offload heavy Unity-hosted computation.
steps: Identify the public host facade and verify it is only a lifecycle gateway.
List runtime phases and the responsibility of each phase.
Identify all structural mutation windows and confirm they are command-controlled.
Map storage identity, dense location, stale-handle protection, and compaction policy.
Map task types and the ownership rules each task must obey.
Verify synchronization boundaries before published state reaches host systems.
Check diagnostics coverage for phase cost, worker utilization, storage growth, and validation failures.

recipes.name: Move Unity main-thread work into runtime phases
recipes.when: Use when a Unity project has heavy gameplay or simulation work that should no longer live directly in MonoBehaviour update logic.
steps: Capture host inputs into runtime-owned snapshots.
Queue mutation intent instead of mutating runtime state directly from Unity code.
Run heavy compute in fixed or variable runtime phases with explicit ownership.
Publish stable outputs after barrier and commit boundaries.
Let Unity adapters consume published payloads during submit or host handoff.
Measure main-thread cost before and after the boundary change.

recipes.name: Validate multithreaded runtime scaling
recipes.when: Use when adding or reviewing worker execution for a CPU simulation runtime.
steps: Keep single-thread mode as the reference path.
Define range ownership, write policy, and reduction merge contract.
Add barriers before any observation of computed results.
Run deterministic comparisons between single-thread and worker execution.
Measure worker utilization, planning cost, reduction commit cost, and imbalance.
Prefer workload reshaping over scheduler complexity if scaling is poor.

pitfalls: Treating Unity as the runtime instead of the host.
Adding multithreading before mutation and storage ownership are disciplined.
Optimizing the scheduler while storage layout still causes scattered access.
Publishing partial worker state to host systems before commit boundaries.
Benchmarking only synthetic load and missing realistic workload behavior.
Letting optional rendering, overlay, or GPU integration leak into core runtime rules.
review_checklist: Is the runtime identity CPU-first, deterministic, and simulation/compute oriented?
Are lifecycle, phases, commands, storage, scheduler, diagnostics, and host boundary separate?
Are structural mutations limited to explicit command/lifecycle windows?
Can hot data be traversed densely by range tasks?
Can single-thread and worker execution be compared deterministically?
Are barriers, reductions, commit, publish, and submit boundaries explicit?
Do diagnostics expose phase cost, worker utilization, storage churn, contention, and backlog?
Do integrations consume published state without controlling core execution?

references.title: Runtime Performance Architecture
references.interpretation: Use this first for CPU-first runtime performance architecture, phase discipline, command mutation, scheduler/storage boundaries, and Unity host separation.
references.why: It captures the domain's top-level DOP-derived performance lens.

references.title: Runtime Synchronization Contract
references.interpretation: Use when worker execution, snapshots, barriers, commit/publish, or host handoff are in scope.
references.why: Parallel performance is invalid without explicit synchronization ownership.

references.title: Performance Benchmarking And Workloads
references.interpretation: Use when comparing single-thread, worker policies, synthetic stress, and realistic workload probes.
references.why: Runtime performance needs both controlled stress and real workload validation.
